title: Neoporteria neohankeana
intro: Neoporteria neohankeana, also known as Chuño or Chilean Wine Palm, is a type of succulent plant native to the Atacama Desert in Chile. Traditionally used in folk medicine, Neoporteria neohankeana has been found to possess various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in wound healing, reducing fever, and alleviating respiratory issues. The plant's bioactive constituents include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In Chilean folk medicine, Neoporteria neohankeana is typically prepared as a tea or infusion, and is also used to make a traditional wine, known as "Chuño", which is believed to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects.
taxonomy: Neoporteria neohankeana is a species of flowering plant in the family Cactaceae. It is native to Chile and is often classified as a type of cactus. The classification of Neoporteria neohankeana is still a subject of ongoing research and debate among botanists. Some sources group it under the genus Neoporteria, while others place it in the genus Pediocactus. Further classification of this plant may require more extensive study and verification of its characteristics.
